sections.text: Welcome back to AP Computer Science A. Module Five — writing classes. This is the deepest module of the first half. By the end you'll be able to design your own Java class from scratch — fields, constructor, methods, the works.

So far you've USED Java's built-in classes — String, Math, Random. Now you flip the role. You'll WRITE your own classes. Model anything you want — a Student, a BankAccount, a Dog, a self-driving car. Think of a class as a blueprint, and an object as one specific thing built from that blueprint. One blueprint, infinite buildings.

Four areas today. One — the class versus object distinction. Two — instance variables and constructors, the way an object holds state and gets built. Three — methods, including accessors, mutators, and toString. Four — the this keyword, plus the difference between instance and static. Stick with me — once this lands, every future module gets easier.

Start with the core idea. A class is a blueprint. It defines what an object will look like and what it can do. An object is one specific instance built from that blueprint. Imagine a cookie cutter and a cookie. One cutter, but you can stamp out as many cookies as you want. One class, infinite objects.

Inside a class you declare instance variables. These hold the state of each object. Here's a Dog class with two fields — a String called name and an int called age. Notice the keyword private in front of each one. Private means encapsulation — code outside the class cannot reach in and touch these fields directly. That's a feature, not a restriction.

Next — the constructor. A constructor is the special method that builds an object. It has the same name as the class, and no return type — not even void. This one takes a String and an int, and assigns them to the instance variables. To use it: Dog d equals new Dog, paren, quote Rex quote, comma three, close paren. The keyword new triggers the constructor.

Methods are the actions a class can perform. Here's a bark method on the Dog class. The word void means the method doesn't return anything — it just prints. Other methods can return an int, a String, or any type you want — you put the return type before the method name. Methods are how an object behaves in the world.

Two special kinds of methods come up constantly. First — accessors, also called getters. An accessor returns the value of a private field so outside code can read it. Here's getName — it returns the name field. Use it like this: String n equals d dot getName, paren, close paren. Read-only access to encapsulated data.

Second — mutators, also called setters. A mutator changes the value of a private field. Here's setAge — it takes an int parameter and assigns it to the age field. Use it like this: d dot setAge of four. Setters give controlled write access. You can even add validation inside the setter — reject negative ages, for example — which is why encapsulation matters.

Here's a common situation. The constructor parameter has the same name as the field — both called name. Now an assignment like name equals name does nothing useful — both sides refer to the parameter. The fix: write this dot name equals name. This dot name means the field of the current object. The plain name on the right side is the parameter. Use this whenever a parameter shadows a field.

Your turn. Write a Student class with two fields — a String name and a double gpa. Add a constructor that takes both as parameters. Add a getName accessor. Pause now, write the full class skeleton, press play when you're ready.

Walking through it. Public class Student. Private String name. Private double gpa. Constructor: public Student, paren, String n, comma double g, close paren — inside, name equals n, gpa equals g. Then the accessor: public String getName, paren, close paren — return name. Private fields, public constructor, public accessor. That's the standard shape of an AP CS A class.

One more method you'll write on almost every class — toString. It returns a String representation of the object, and Java calls it automatically when you println an object. Here's a toString for Dog — it returns the name plus age in parentheses. Now System dot out dot println of d prints Rex paren three close paren. Clean, readable, debug-friendly.

Now the static keyword. Static means class-level, not instance-level. A static method belongs to the class itself, not to any specific object. You've already used this — Math dot random is static. You call it on the class Math, not on an instance of Math. Use static for utility methods and for counters shared across all instances of the class.

Side by side. To call an instance method you need an object — d dot bark, paren, close paren. To call a static method you don't — Math dot random, paren, close paren. The difference comes from one keyword. Add static to a method's declaration and it becomes class-level. Skip it and it's instance-level. AP exam loves testing this distinction.

Wrong versus right. The most common compile error in this module is forgetting the return type. Public getName with no return type will not compile — Java has no idea what type the method returns. The fix is public String getName. Same with the method call — never forget the parentheses. getName with no parens is a field reference, not a method call. Two tiny details, two huge causes of red squiggles.

Recap. A class is a blueprint, an object is an instance. Private fields plus public methods equals encapsulation. The constructor builds, methods do. The this keyword resolves name shadowing. toString controls how your object prints. Static is class-level, instance is object-level. Your assignment is in the dashboard — write a BankAccount class with a balance field, deposit and withdraw methods, a getBalance accessor, and a toString.

This video is the overview. To master writing classes you need reps. Work through the Writing Classes chapter on Runestone — interactive code blocks. Hit the Codingbat AP-1 and Class-design problem sets. Submit the BankAccount assignment in your dashboard. And if anything is still fuzzy, book a check-in with your advisor — twenty minutes here saves you hours later. See you in Module Six — Array.
